region_of_origin: Guangdong Province, China; also popular in Singapore and Malaysia

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Hakka Abacus Beads is approximately 250 g, equivalent to one plate served as a main dish at lunch or dinner.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Taro, cooked (SR Legacy ID 11514) and Pork, ground, cooked (SR Legacy ID 10873); composite estimation for Hakka Abacus Beads
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Hakka Abacus Beads exists in USDA FoodData Central, HPB Singapore, or IMR Malaysia MyFCD. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy entries for cooked taro (FNDDS 11514), tapioca (FNDDS 20120), ground pork cooked (FNDDS 10873), dried shrimp (FNDDS 15152), and shiitake mushrooms (FNDDS 11238), weighted by approximate recipe proportions (taro+tapioca ~55%, pork ~20%, dried seafood ~10%, mushrooms/fungus ~10%, oil+seasonings ~5%). Singapore HPB database consulted but no direct match found. Input AI-generated values (confidence 0.9) are consistent with this composite estimate and have been retained with source upgraded to USDA composite estimation. Sodium value of 380 mg/100 g is plausible given dried shrimp, fish sauce, and soy sauce; consistent with USDA seasoned pork dish analogues.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of taro (Colocasia esculenta) and taro-based composite dishes — analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database entry for taro, boiled
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Hakka Abacus Beads. GI of 52 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled taro (GI ~53, medium-low), adjusted downward slightly to 52 to account for the moderating effect of co-ingested protein (pork, dried shrimp) and dietary fibre (mushrooms, wood ear fungus) on glycemic response, consistent with mixed-meal GI attenuation principles documented in peer-reviewed literature (Augustin et al., 2015, Am J Clin Nutr). Glycemic load per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (52 × 81.25) / 100 = 42.3 g GL per 250 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (380 mg/100 g; ~950 mg per 250 g serving) from dried shrimp, fish sauce, and soy sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 100–150 g and request reduced-sodium seasoning, as a standard 250 g serving provides approximately 950 mg sodium — nearly 40% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (GL ~42 per serving) from taro and tapioca flour base, partially offset by protein and fibre content
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes may consume Hakka Abacus Beads in controlled portions (100–150 g) as the low GI of taro (GI ~52) and co-ingested protein and fibre help moderate postprandial glucose rise, but the high tapioca flour content means carbohydrate load remains significant at full serving size.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from pork and cooking oil; partially offset by potassium (280 mg/100 g) and low sugar content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption due to high sodium content and moderate saturated fat (~1.8 g/100 g); choosing smaller portions and requesting less oil and low-sodium soy sauce can reduce cardiovascular risk while preserving the dish's nutritional benefits from taro and mushrooms.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Taro is non-inflammatory; wood ear fungus and shiitake mushrooms provide anti-inflammatory polysaccharides
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Hakka Abacus Beads is generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis as taro is a non-inflammatory starch and the mushroom components provide beneficial beta-glucan polysaccharides; no specific contraindications identified.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hakka Abacus Beads (算盘子, Suan Pan Zi) is a traditional and highly labour-intensive Hakka Chinese dish originating from Guangdong Province, China, and widely enjoyed in Singapore and Malaysia. The dish consists of small, chewy, disc-shaped dumplings made from a dough of mashed steamed taro (Colocasia esculenta) combined with tapioca flour, hand-shaped to resemble the beads of a traditional Chinese abacus — a shape that symbolises wealth and the counting of money, making this a popular festive and celebratory dish. The beads are first boiled until cooked through, then stir-fried in a wok with a flavourful medley of minced pork, dried shrimp, dried cuttlefish, reconstituted shiitake mushrooms, and wood ear fungus. The dish is seasoned with soy sauce, fish sauce, and white pepper, producing a complex, deeply savoury, umami-rich flavour profile with a satisfying chewy-springy texture from the taro-tapioca beads contrasted against the tender pork and silky fungus. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 210 kcal, 4.5 g protein, 6.8 g fat, and 32.5 g carbohydrate, with a low glycemic index of approximately 52 attributable to the taro base and the moderating effect of co-ingested protein and fibre. Sodium content is high at 380 mg/100 g due to dried seafood and fermented seasonings, making portion control important for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is a good source of potassium (280 mg/100 g), magnesium (35 mg/100 g), and B vitamins. It is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with shellfish, mollusc, soy, or fish allergies. Clinically, it is best consumed in moderate portions by individuals managing blood pressure or blood glucose.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Hakka Abacus Beads exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore, USDA FoodData Central, IMR Malaysia MyFCD). Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy composite of cooked taro, tapioca, ground pork, dried shrimp, and shiitake mushrooms weighted by approximate recipe proportions. Input AI-generated values (confidence 0.9) are consistent with this composite estimate. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled taro. Confidence is therefore rated medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked taro (ID 11514), tapioca (ID 20120), ground pork cooked (ID 10873), dried shrimp (ID 15152), and shiitake mushrooms (ID 11238), weighted by approximate recipe proportions. No exact approved-source entry for Hakka Abacus Beads was found in HPB Singapore, USDA FoodData Central, or IMR Malaysia MyFCD as of 2026-04-16. 2. Saturated fat (1.8 g/100 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (2.9 g/100 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.5 g/100 g), trans_fat_g (0.0 g/100 g), cholesterol_mg (18 mg/100 g), and water_g (52 g/100 g) are estimated from USDA composite; these fields were not present in the input record and have been added. 3. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database (boiled taro GI ~53), adjusted for mixed-meal protein and fibre attenuation. 4. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (52 × 81.25) / 100 = 42.3 g. 5. The dish contains crustaceans (dried shrimp), molluscs (dried cuttlefish), soy, fish, and gluten allergens and is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans. 6. Halal status is uncertain as fish sauce and soy sauce brands vary; marked is_halal: false pending verification of specific product ingredients. 7. Duplicate portion entries in the input record have been deduplicated to a single 250 g serving. 8. FoodOn and FoodEx2 ontology codes are analogous estimates; no exact codes exist for this dish in either catalogue as of 2026. 9. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g were not present in the input record; values have been estimated from USDA composite sources and documented accordingly.
